ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic curry rice dish class; no beef-specific Japanese curry rice entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers curry rice as a composite dish; no beef-specific Japanese curry rice sub-code exists in the published hierarchy. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for beef curry rice identified in the published clinical terminology hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of beef curry rice served in a Japanese curry restaurant or hawker stall, approximately 350 g, comprising steamed white rice topped with beef and vegetable curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as-is from the HPB Foodo database entry for Beef Curry Rice (G00010217). Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef curry (NDB No. 22904) as a secondary reference; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based curry dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Japanese beef curry rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated using the analogous food 'white rice with curry sauce' (GI ~54, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The curry sauce and vegetable components lower the effective GI of plain white rice (GI ~72) through fat, protein, and fibre dilution. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (54) × carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 350 g serving = 88.2 g) / 100 = 47.6; adjusted to per-serving GL = 54 × 67.4 g available carbohydrate / 100 = 36.4 (using dietary fibre subtraction: 88.2 − 13.0 g fibre = 75.2 g; conservative estimate applied).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced-sodium curry sauce; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K vs 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets vascular risk, but cumulative sodium from soy sauce or commercial curry roux can be substantial.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g; estimated GI 54 (low); a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 88 g total carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to 200–250 g, substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic impact, and monitor post-prandial blood glucose, as the low-medium GI and high carbohydrate load per serving can cause significant glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 1.7 g/100 g from beef and curry roux; sodium 231 mg/100 g; cholesterol reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB (likely a rounding artefact for a beef-containing dish).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean beef cuts, limit portion to 200 g, and select low-sodium curry roux to manage combined saturated fat and sodium exposure; the 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre content provides modest cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which drives pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA pathogenesis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with oily fish, chicken, or legumes to reduce arachidonic acid load; turmeric-based curry spices may provide minor anti-inflammatory benefit but are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory effect of regular red meat consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Curry Rice (ビーフカレーライス, Biifu Karee Raisu) is a Japanese-style composite dish consisting of steamed white rice served alongside or topped with a thick, mildly spiced curry sauce containing braised beef, onion, carrot, and potato. Japanese curry is characteristically milder, sweeter, and thicker than South Asian curries, typically prepared using a proprietary curry roux block (containing wheat flour, fat, and a blend of spices including turmeric, cumin, and coriander). The dish is a staple of Japanese home cooking and is widely served in Japanese curry restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore. Per 100 g, it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate with 3.7 g dietary fibre, making it a moderate-energy, moderate-carbohydrate dish. The sodium content of 231 mg/100 g is clinically relevant for hypertension management. The estimated glycaemic index of 54 (low) reflects the moderating effect of fat, protein, and fibre on the glycaemic response of the white rice base. The dish is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of beef. It is appropriate for lunch or dinner and is culturally associated with Japanese cuisine as served in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all fields. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy as a secondary reference and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food entry with documented basis. All mandatory Japanese-language naming requirements satisfied.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.7 g/100 g) is not reported in the HPB source for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest match for beef curry dish and documented as adapted. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB beef curry rice entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB; this is likely a rounding artefact for a beef-containing dish and is preserved as reported from the approved source. This value is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry G00010217. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice with curry sauce; no direct measurement for Japanese beef curry rice exists in the approved GI database. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry for Japanese beef curry rice exists in the published hierarchies as of 2026-04-16.
